summary: Organizations systematically fail by treating individuals as fungible units rather than recognizing that individual effectiveness varies by orders of magnitude. This manifests in roadmap planning where teams privately know projects will fail based on who's assigned, in HR systems that force headcount-based budgeting preventing hiring of exceptional talent, and in attrition management where 'normalizing' turnover disproportionately drives out the most effective employees. The cost of this legibility-driven approach is enormous in heavy-tailed distributions of talent, where the right individual can single-handedly increase a giant company's revenue by 0.7% or solve decades-old problems in foreign aid work.
key_points: Roadmap planning treats people as interchangeable, but teams privately predict project success/failure based on who's actually assigned
HR systems convert budgets into fixed 'headcount' at standardized compensation levels, preventing hiring of highly effective or specialized talent
Companies that artificially increase attrition to 'healthy' levels lose their most effective engineers at 2x+ the average rate
High-impact interventions (in both tech and development work) are often not amenable to simple quantitative analysis and depend heavily on team composition
Prioritizing legibility and scalable processes over recognizing individual variance is extremely costly in heavy-tailed distributions of talent
money_quotes: I've never worked in an organization where this actually worked, where this actually enabled teams to effectively execute with dependencies on other teams.

People, as a whole, cannot be treated as an abstraction where the actions company leadership takes impacts everyone in the same way. The people who are most effective will be disproportionately likely to leave if you turn a knob that leads to increased attrition.
Unfortunately for those people, as well as for the world, the world is not simple.

When ROI is a heavy-tailed distribution, when the right person can, on their paternity leave, increase company revenue of a giant tech company by 0.7% and then much more when they work on that full-time, then severely tamping down on the right side of the curve to improve legibility is very costly and can cost you the majority of your potential returns.
Many people want the world to be simple and apply the algorithm 'charity on GiveWell list = good; not on GiveWill list = bad' because it makes the world simple for them.
key_insight: Treating individuals as interchangeable for organizational legibility and scalability systematically destroys value in systems with heavy-tailed talent distributions, where single exceptional individuals can create orders of magnitude more impact than average performers.
